([/ˈskuːnmeɪkər/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English");[[1]](#cite_note-1) born January 3, 1940) is an American [film editor](/wiki/Film_editor "Film editor"), best known for her collaboration over five decades with director [Martin Scorsese](/wiki/Martin_Scorsese "Martin Scorsese"). She has received numerous accolades, including three [Academy Awards](/wiki/Academy_Awards "Academy Awards"), two [BAFTA Awards](/wiki/British_Academy_Film_Awards "British Academy Film Awards"), and four [ACE Eddie Awards](/wiki/American_Cinema_Editors "American Cinema Editors"). She has been honored with the [British Film Institute Fellowship](/wiki/British_Film_Institute_Fellowship "British Film Institute Fellowship") in 1997, the [Golden Lion for Lifetime Achievement](/wiki/Golden_Lion_for_Lifetime_Achievement "Golden Lion for Lifetime Achievement") in 2014, and the [BAFTA Fellowship](/wiki/BAFTA_Fellowship "BAFTA Fellowship") in 2019.

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

American film editor (born 1940)

| Thelma Schoonmaker | |
| --- | --- |
| Schoonmaker in 2023 | |
| Born | Thelma Colbert Schoonmaker   (1940-01-03) January 3, 1940 (age 86)  [Algiers](/wiki/Algiers "Algiers"), [French Algeria](/wiki/French_Algeria "French Algeria") |
| Alma mater | [Cornell University](/wiki/Cornell_University "Cornell University") |
| Occupation | [Film editor](/wiki/Film_editor "Film editor") |

**Thelma Schoonmaker** ([/ˈskuːnmeɪkər/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English");[[1]](#cite_note-1) born January 3, 1940) is an American [film editor](/wiki/Film_editor "Film editor"), best known for her collaboration over five decades with director [Martin Scorsese](/wiki/Martin_Scorsese "Martin Scorsese"). She has received numerous accolades, including three [Academy Awards](/wiki/Academy_Awards "Academy Awards"), two [BAFTA Awards](/wiki/British_Academy_Film_Awards "British Academy Film Awards"), and four [ACE Eddie Awards](/wiki/American_Cinema_Editors "American Cinema Editors"). She has been honored with the [British Film Institute Fellowship](/wiki/British_Film_Institute_Fellowship "British Film Institute Fellowship") in 1997, the [Golden Lion for Lifetime Achievement](/wiki/Golden_Lion_for_Lifetime_Achievement "Golden Lion for Lifetime Achievement") in 2014, and the [BAFTA Fellowship](/wiki/BAFTA_Fellowship "BAFTA Fellowship") in 2019.

Schoonmaker started working with Scorsese on his debut feature film, *[Who's That Knocking at My Door](/wiki/Who%27s_That_Knocking_at_My_Door "Who's That Knocking at My Door")* (1967), and has edited all of his films since *[Raging Bull](/wiki/Raging_Bull "Raging Bull")* (1980). She has received a record nine nominations for the [Academy Award for Best Film Editing](/wiki/Academy_Award_for_Best_Film_Editing "Academy Award for Best Film Editing") and has won a record three times for *Raging Bull*, *[The Aviator](/wiki/The_Aviator_(2004_film) "The Aviator (2004 film)")* (2004), and *[The Departed](/wiki/The_Departed "The Departed")* (2006). She has also been nominated for the [BAFTA Award for Best Editing](/wiki/BAFTA_Award_for_Best_Editing "BAFTA Award for Best Editing") a record 11 times, winning twice for *Raging Bull* and *[Goodfellas](/wiki/Goodfellas "Goodfellas")* (1990).

## Early life

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Thelma_Schoonmaker&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Early life")]

Thelma Schoonmaker was born on January 3, 1940, in [Algiers](/wiki/Algiers "Algiers") (then part of [French Algeria](/wiki/French_Algeria "French Algeria")), the daughter of American parents, Thelma and Bertram Schoonmaker.[[2]](#cite_note-LagoColony-2)[[3]](#cite_note-Meuel_2016-3) Bertram, descended from the New York Dutch [Schoonmaker](/wiki/Schoonmaker "Schoonmaker") political family,[[3]](#cite_note-Meuel_2016-3) was employed as an agent of the [Standard Oil Company](/wiki/Standard_Oil "Standard Oil") and worked extensively abroad.[[4]](#cite_note-Greencine-4) The Schoonmakers were evacuated to the United States shortly after the [Fall of France](/wiki/Battle_of_France "Battle of France") during the [Second World War](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II").[[2]](#cite_note-LagoColony-2) In 1941, the family moved to the Dutch-[Caribbean](/wiki/Caribbean "Caribbean") island of [Aruba](/wiki/Aruba "Aruba"),[[2]](#cite_note-LagoColony-2)[[4]](#cite_note-Greencine-4) where Schoonmaker's father continued to work for Standard Oil and her mother ran nursery schools.[[5]](#cite_note-VillageVoice-5) Schoonmaker was primarily raised in Aruba, in a community she described as "a colony of expatriates from over the world";[[5]](#cite_note-VillageVoice-5) she also spent part of her childhood in Portugal.[[6]](#cite_note-ElleAnderson-6)

Schoonmaker did not live in the United States until she was an adolescent in 1955, and was initially alienated and dumbfounded by American culture.[[4]](#cite_note-Greencine-4) She settled in [Ridgewood, New Jersey](/wiki/Ridgewood,_New_Jersey "Ridgewood, New Jersey")[[7]](#cite_note-7) and graduated in 1957 from [Ridgewood High School](/wiki/Ridgewood_High_School_(New_Jersey) "Ridgewood High School (New Jersey)").[[8]](#cite_note-8) Schoonmaker was interested in a career in international diplomacy and began attending [Cornell University](/wiki/Cornell_University "Cornell University") in 1957, where she studied [political science](/wiki/Political_science "Political science") and the [Russian language](/wiki/Russian_language "Russian language"). When she graduated from Cornell in 1961, she began taking [State Department](/wiki/United_States_Department_of_State "United States Department of State") tests in order to apply for positions within the [U.S. government](/wiki/Federal_government_of_the_United_States "Federal government of the United States").[[4]](#cite_note-Greencine-4)[[9]](#cite_note-Cornell-9) Politically inclined and opinionated, Schoonmaker was [opposed to the Vietnam War](/wiki/Opposition_to_United_States_involvement_in_the_Vietnam_War "Opposition to United States involvement in the Vietnam War") and supported the [Civil rights movement](/wiki/Civil_rights_movement "Civil rights movement").[[5]](#cite_note-VillageVoice-5) She passed the State Department exams but failed the final "stress test" when she expressed distaste for the South African policy of [apartheid](/wiki/Apartheid "Apartheid"), a stance which did not sit well with those administering the tests.[[4]](#cite_note-Greencine-4)[[10]](#cite_note-TelegraphShoard-10) The State Department [claimed](https://www.theguardian.com/film/2023/dec/08/thelma-schoonmaker-interview-the-red-shoes-michael-powell-martin-scorsese) that her views were "too politically liberal" and that she wouldn't be happy having a job at the Department.

## Career

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Thelma_Schoonmaker&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Career")]

> You get to contribute so significantly in the editing room because you shape the movie and the performances. You help the director bring all the hard work of those who made the film to fruition. You give their work rhythm and pace and sometimes adjust the structure to make the film work – to make it start to flow up there on the screen. And then it's very rewarding after a year's work to see people react to what you've done in the theater.

—Thelma Schoonmaker, on editing[[11]](#cite_note-iVillage-11)

While taking a graduate course in [primitive art](/wiki/Primitive_art "Primitive art") at [Columbia University](/wiki/Columbia_University "Columbia University"), Schoonmaker saw an advertisement in *[The New York Times](/wiki/The_New_York_Times "The New York Times")* that offered training as an assistant [film editor](/wiki/Film_editor "Film editor").[[12]](#cite_note-12)[[13]](#cite_note-HollywoodAward-13) She responded to the advertisement and got the job. The job entailed assisting an "[editor](/wiki/Film_editor "Film editor")" who was randomly cutting frames from classic European films (such as those by [Truffaut](/wiki/Fran%C3%A7ois_Truffaut "François Truffaut"), [Godard](/wiki/Jean-Luc_Godard "Jean-Luc Godard") and [Fellini](/wiki/Federico_Fellini "Federico Fellini")), so that their length would conform to the running times of U.S. television broadcasts.[[4]](#cite_note-Greencine-4)

She signed up for a brief six-week course in filmmaking at [New York University](/wiki/New_York_University "New York University"), where she came into contact with young [Martin Scorsese](/wiki/Martin_Scorsese "Martin Scorsese"), who was struggling to complete his short film *[What's a Nice Girl Like You Doing in a Place Like This?](/wiki/What%27s_a_Nice_Girl_Like_You_Doing_in_a_Place_Like_This%3F "What's a Nice Girl Like You Doing in a Place Like This?")*. A [negative cutter](/wiki/Negative_cutter "Negative cutter") had butchered the film, not leaving enough negative frames to allow for hot splicing, so a film professor asked Schoonmaker to help Scorsese.[[4]](#cite_note-Greencine-4) Schoonmaker went on to edit Scorsese's feature directorial debut, *[Who's That Knocking at My Door](/wiki/Who%27s_That_Knocking_at_My_Door "Who's That Knocking at My Door")* (1967).[[14]](#cite_note-14)

Schoonmaker received her first major screen credits when she and Scorsese both became part of the editing team on [Michael Wadleigh](/wiki/Michael_Wadleigh "Michael Wadleigh")'s seminal music festival [documentary](/wiki/Documentary_film "Documentary film"), 1970's *[Woodstock](/wiki/Woodstock_(film) "Woodstock (film)")*.[[15]](#cite_note-Collider.com-15) She received an Oscar nomination for Best Film Editing for her groundbreaking work—the first documentary ever to be nominated in that category.[[16]](#cite_note-DFOtherCats-16) Her use of [superimpositions](/wiki/Superimposition "Superimposition") and [freeze frames](/wiki/Freeze_frame_shot "Freeze frame shot") brought the performances in the film to life and added to the movie's broad appeal, thus helping to raise the artistry and visibility of documentary film-making to a new level.[[13]](#cite_note-HollywoodAward-13)

The early period of Schoonmaker's career was difficult. Despite being an Oscar nominee, Schoonmaker could not work on feature films unless she became a member of the [Motion Picture Editors Guild](/wiki/Motion_Picture_Editors_Guild "Motion Picture Editors Guild").[[5]](#cite_note-VillageVoice-5) The union's entry requirements included spending five years as an apprentice and three as an assistant, which Schoonmaker was unwilling to meet.[[5]](#cite_note-VillageVoice-5)[[11]](#cite_note-iVillage-11)[[17]](#cite_note-FT.com-17) Schoonmaker remarked, "And I just couldn't see why I, who had been a full editor and had been nominated for an Academy Award, should suddenly have to become an apprentice. ...And of course, *they* couldn't see the sense of why I, who had never been in the union all those years and had never paid dues all those years and had never served my time in their sense, should be allowed as a full editor. So it was quite understandable on both sides. It was just insane."[[5]](#cite_note-VillageVoice-5)

Consequently, Schoonmaker did not work with Scorsese in a formal capacity in the 1970s;[[17]](#cite_note-FT.com-17) however, she did make an uncredited contribution to *[Taxi Driver](/wiki/Taxi_Driver "Taxi Driver")*. Scorsese had decided not to edit the picture during principal photography, but to save all the editing until shooting had wrapped. Unfortunately, this left him very little time to cut the picture, as Columbia's contract stipulated that a finished cut had to be supplied by the middle of February. Scorsese brought in Schoonmaker to help. At one point, [Steven Spielberg](/wiki/Steven_Spielberg "Steven Spielberg") visited Scorsese and chipped in with some contributions toward the final edit.[[18]](#cite_note-Sangster_2002-18)[[19]](#cite_note-VarietyKowalski-19)

In the 1980s, Schoonmaker, with some help from Scorsese, was eventually accepted into the union. They worked together on the classic sports drama *[Raging Bull](/wiki/Raging_Bull "Raging Bull")*, which is widely considered masterful editing and won her the Best Film Editing Oscar.[[20]](#cite_note-20) During her acceptance speech for the film, Schoonmaker [said](https://aframe.oscars.org/what-to-watch/post/thelma-schoonmaker-top-5-exclusive) "I want to thank, first of all, Marty Scorsese; he edited this film with me every minute of the time. I want to thank him particularly for his brilliant direction, and Robert De Niro for his incredible performance which gave me gold to work with — pure gold."

## Personal life

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Thelma_Schoonmaker&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Personal life")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/aa/ThelmaSchoonmaker.jpg/250px-ThelmaSchoonmaker.jpg)](/wiki/File:ThelmaSchoonmaker.jpg)

Thelma Schoonmaker and Columba Powell at the [Cannes Film Festival](/wiki/Cannes_Film_Festival "Cannes Film Festival") (2009). Columba Powell is the son of [Michael Powell](/wiki/Michael_Powell "Michael Powell"), a prominent film director to whom Schoonmaker was married.

She was introduced to [Michael Powell](/wiki/Michael_Powell "Michael Powell") by Scorsese and London-based film producer Frixos Constantine.[[17]](#cite_note-FT.com-17) The couple were married from May 19, 1984, until his death in 1990. The couple had no children.[[21]](#cite_note-TimeOut-21)

Since Powell's death, Schoonmaker has dedicated herself[[22]](#cite_note-22) to preserving the films and honoring the legacy of her husband, who directed many classic films with his partner, Emeric Pressburger. In an [interview](https://aframe.oscars.org/what-to-watch/post/thelma-schoonmaker-top-5-exclusive) with A.Frame asking Schoonmaker what her five favorite films of all time are, she listed two of their films, *[The Red Shoes](/wiki/The_Red_Shoes_(1948_film) "The Red Shoes (1948 film)")* and *[The Life and Death of Colonel Blimp](/wiki/The_Life_and_Death_of_Colonel_Blimp "The Life and Death of Colonel Blimp")*. (The others were *[Faces](/wiki/Faces_(1968_film) "Faces (1968 film)")*, *[The Flowers of St. Francis](/wiki/The_Flowers_of_St._Francis "The Flowers of St. Francis")* and *[Raging Bull](/wiki/Raging_Bull "Raging Bull")*.)

## Achievements and recognition

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Thelma_Schoonmaker&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Achievements and recognition")]

Schoonmaker equaled the record for the most Oscar wins (three) in the [Best Film Editing](/wiki/Academy_Award_for_Best_Film_Editing "Academy Award for Best Film Editing") category, shared with [Ralph Dawson](/wiki/Ralph_Dawson "Ralph Dawson"), [Daniel Mandell](/wiki/Daniel_Mandell "Daniel Mandell"), and [Michael Kahn](/wiki/Michael_Kahn_(film_editor) "Michael Kahn (film editor)").[[23]](#cite_note-23) Furthermore, she holds the record for most nominations in that category, with nine.[[24]](#cite_note-24) Schoonmaker is also the first woman to win multiple Oscars for film editing.[[25]](#cite_note-25)

In 2012, on the 75th anniversary of its founding, the [Motion Picture Editors Guild](/wiki/Motion_Picture_Editors_Guild "Motion Picture Editors Guild") issued a list of the 75 best-edited films of all time based on a survey of its membership. Three films edited by Schoonmaker made the list—all directed by Scorsese and spanning three different decades—including *Raging Bull* (1980), which is ranked first; *Goodfellas* (1990), ranked fifteenth; and *Hugo* (2011), ranked sixty-ninth.[[26]](#cite_note-26) Only [George Tomasini](/wiki/George_Tomasini "George Tomasini"), the editor of [Alfred Hitchcock](/wiki/Alfred_Hitchcock "Alfred Hitchcock")'s films in the 1950s and 1960s, has more appearances on this list, with four; and only [Dede Allen](/wiki/Dede_Allen "Dede Allen") also edited three pictures across three decades, with hers in particular from the 1960s through the 1980s.[[27]](#cite_note-27)
